concepts.title: Rate Constant (k) is independent of Concentration
concepts.description: The rate constant (k) of a reaction is independent of the concentration of reactants. It depends only on temperature and is a measure of how fast a reaction occurs.

concepts.title: Rate of Reaction
concepts.description: Rate of reaction is defined as the change in concentration of reactants or product per unit time.

concepts.title: Activation Energy (Ea)
concepts.description: Activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. It is the energy barrier that must be overcome for the reaction to proceed.

concepts.title: Order of Reaction
concepts.description: To remember the order of reaction, use the acronym 'RATE': R - Reaction, A - Amount, T - Time, E - Energy.

concepts.title: Confusing Rate and Rate Constant
concepts.description: Students often confuse rate and rate constant. Rate is a measure of how fast a reaction occurs, while rate constant is a measure of how fast a reaction occurs at a given temperature.

concepts.title: Units of Rate Constant (k)
concepts.description: The units of rate constant (k) depend on the order of reaction. For a first-order reaction, the units are s^-1, for a second-order reaction, the units are L/mol/s, and for a third-order reaction, the units are L^2/mol^2/s.

concepts.title: Arrhenius Equation
concepts.description: The Arrhenius equation relates the rate constant (k) to temperature (T) and activation energy (Ea): k = Ae^(-Ea/RT), where A is the frequency factor, Ea is the activation energy, R is the gas constant, and T is the temperature in Kelvin.

concepts.title: Collision Theory
concepts.description: Collision theory states that a reaction occurs when two reactant molecules collide with each other with sufficient energy and proper orientation.
